title: Collections: Why Are There No Empires in Age of Empires?
excerpt: At long last, Relic Entertainment has announced that Age of Empires 4 is coming. Strategy gamers rejoice! I am excited – I played the first one back in ’98 (I may be dating myself here)…
content_html:
At long last, Relic Entertainment has announced that Age of Empires 4 is coming. Strategy gamers rejoice! I am excited – I played the first one back in ’98 (I may be dating myself here).

But the news brought me back to a common problem with many games of this type and with Age of Empires specifically: they don’t actually have any empires in them! And I am sure that some of you are a bit confused by this statement – after all, the games are full of Romans and Mongols and Persians and Assyrians and how can I say these weren’t empires? But there’s the thing – those were peoples who had empires. And it turns out that’s a pretty important distinction.

So today, we’re going to talk about what empire is, and (on a very basic level) how they work to see how games of this sort – not just AoE, but also the Civilization series and others – don’t reflect the mechanics of actual empires and why that matters.

What Is an Empire?

Let’s not bury the lede too far: an empire is a state where the core ruling population exercises control and extracts resources from a periphery which is composed of people other than the core group (linguistically/culturally/ethnically/religiously distinct). So an empire is a state where one set of people (the core) extract resources (typically by force) from another set of people (the periphery).

That definition goes back to the root of the word in Latin: imperium, literally meaning a command or control; imperium comes from the Latin verb imperio (lit: ‘to order or command’). Thus imperium was a sphere of command over others. In Roman politics, this could mean an individual had the authority to command an army or to set up courts (consuls, praetors and dictators had this sort of imperium), but the Romans understood their empire as a sort of command exercised by the Senate and People of Rome over non-Roman people, thus they called that too imperium – an imperium of the Roman people (imperium populi Romani), crucially over the non-Roman people; once cannot, after all, have imperium over one’s self. An imperium of the Roman people must be an imperium over someone else.

Contrary to the venerable Wikipedia, empire does not require a monarchy. Rome was an empire while it was still a Republic, and France continued to hold an empire after it stopped being a monarchy. Athens, famously, converted the Delian League into an Athenian Empire (the Greek word used is ἀρχή (‘arche’), pronounced ar-KHAY) while it was still, internally, a democracy. Often, when discussing the internal politics of these states (especially for Rome and France) we will distinguish between a period of ’empire’ and ‘republic’ to note the shift from a republic to a monarchy or vice-versa, but that sort of nomenclature should not be taken to disguise the fact that, for instance, the Roman Republic in 150 B.C. was very much possessed of an empire, while still functioning as a republic.

Empire, I should note, seems to be one of – if not the – dominant form of large-scale human social organization since at least the bronze age (which is to say: since as far back as our sources let us see clearly). Ideas like loose federations of states (e.g. the EU) or nation-states are relatively new; in many cases, our modern nation-states are merely the consolidated form of what were originally empires of various sizes (e.g. China, Russia, but also France (see: Crusade, Albigensian), etc.). We don’t think about them that way anymore, because the steady application of state power created the shared culture that subsequently formed the foundation for the nation (we’ll return to this – the way that how we think about these empires often disguises their nature – in a moment). In many respects, empire is normal (which, please note, does not mean it is good), whereas this modern world composed primarily of nation-states is an unusual aberration.

(As a side note, this to a degree exposes a bit of understandable but still amusing silliness in titling anything Age of Empires. There has never been an age without empires. We are certainly not living in an age without empires even now – ask the Tibetans, the Uighyrs, the Chechens how ‘ended’ imperialism is! And that doesn’t even get into the sticky question of if we might understand the United States as an empire (my own view is that it is better understood as a hegemony – but I could hardly blame a Guamanian, Puerto Rican or Navajo who begged to differ – and that, importantly, it is also not, and has never been, a nation-state, but that’s a contentious topic for another day).

Which brings us back to Age of Empires, but also a slew of similar games which promise something they call ’empire building.’

In a typical game of Age of Empires, you begin with a small group of settlers imagined to be founding some great empire. You begin with workers harvesting local resources, which you use to build buildings which in turn produce further workers and then military units (I am, I know, moving over a lot of complexity in terms of tech-trees, age advancements, cheeky things you can do with long-distance trade, wololo and the rest – I do promise I have played these games, but I want to keep to the relevant details).

As the player expands – pursuing one of several victory conditions – they will run into other players (or the AI simulating players). The default setting is for all players to effectively be hostile to each other. Players do not conqueror the settlements of opposing players, or reduce them to tributaries (nor – since only one player or team can ‘win’ – can they settle on mutual cooperation), instead they annihilate those settlements, replacing whatever resource production they had with villager’s of the players own culture. It is striking that villagers cannot normally be captured (we’ll get to priests in a moment!), only killed.

This is, to put it bluntly, not how empires work. The entire point of establishing an empire is to access the resources and labor of a subordinate population (the periphery) – exterminating that population defeats the very purpose! The ’empires’ in Age of Empires are not empires at all, but fanatically murderous nation-states, projected backwards in history hundreds – if not thousands – of years before any such idea of a state existed. Few states have really followed this vision of conquest, but those that have – Nazi Germany is the most obvious example – are not generally well-thought of.

Now, there are three caveats on that rather scathing statement I want to address. First, that the game is to be understood as an abstraction and that we are to imagine all of the complexities of empire building taking place ‘behind the screen’ as it were – and that’s fair enough for many games of this type (it is surely what Civilization seems to want us to believe), but I don’t think it goes for Age of Empires, since the non-combatants are so clearly on screen and are not capturable by military units.

They are capturable by priests, which leads to the second caveat – that it is possible to forcibly convert, rather than annihilate, a people in Age of Empires. But that has three problems: first, that sort of cultural genocide is not something real empires usually (there were some exceptions) much cared about (because they do not care what the local customs are, generally, so long as the taxes get paid) and second, that shifting from genocide to ‘mere’ ethnic cleansing is not a great improvement. Third, it is clearly not expected that you will do this to most – or even very many – of your opponents. Priests capture one unit at a time with a long cooldown; the vast majority of humans on the map belonging to rival empires will be killed rather than captured in most games. Capture is merely a niche way to neutralize certain high value enemy units and thus turn battles – mechanically, it is not a real alternative to the war-of-annihilation.

The third, predictable and annoying argument is “stop overthinking it, it is just a game.” Which is – if you will permit me to paraphrase C.S. Lewis – an unserious ‘boy’s philosophy’ unfit for adults. Video games are cultural products, like books or films or any other – and to say that those cultural products have no impact or importance is to argue against the historically obvious, given how frequently we find such things at the roots of important political ideologies, social movements, and wars.

Real Empires Have Peripheries

Alright, so Age of Empires falls down on this account, so what?

I think the problem on this point is that Age of Empires – and cultural products like it – can distort our understanding of what empires are, and how they work and also how our own societies work (when we analogize from past ones – one of the core uses of historical reasoning). In particular, it appears in a failure to appreciate that successful empires historically were diverse by their very nature and that being a successful empire depended on managing diversity and benefiting from it.

Let’s take Rome as an example. As an ‘Empire’ in Age of Empires, the Roman player recruits Roman units, destroys their rivals and repopulates the land with Romans (this is not unique to the Romans – all factions function like this in the game). And – I want to make clear this is not a straw-man argument – I have absolutely had well-meaning people quite confidently inform me that Roman military strength was a consequence of their cultural and ethnic cohesiveness. I am not saying they learned this from video games, but rather than their vision and that of a game like Age of Empires seem to coincide and I suspect arise from the same set of misperceptions (yet another word that spellcheck defiantly insists is not a word, even though it is (I link to Websters because the OED is behind a paywall, but rest assured, misperception is there too)).

The Age of Empire version of Rome is thus always completely Roman, through and through, and never more powerful than the number of ethnic Romans on the map.

The actual Roman Empire was fantastically diverse and more importantly, its military success hinged on its diversity at every stage of its existence. In many games and cultural products, that diversity is obscured because we lose sight of ethnic, religious, linguistic and cultural divisions which were very important at the time, but no longer matter to us very much. Let’s take a snapshot of Roman territory in 218 B.C. to give a sense of this.

Quite a few people look at a map like that, classify most of Rome’s territories as ‘Italian’ and assume there is a large, homogeneous ethnic core there (except, I suspect, anyone who has actually been to Italy and is aware that Italy is hardly homogeneous, even today!). But Roman Italy in 218 B.C. was nothing like that.

Peninsular Italy (which doesn’t include the Po River Valley) contained a bewildering array of cultures and peoples: at least three distinct religious systems (Roman, Etruscan, Greek), half a dozen languages (some completely unrelated to each other) and many clearly distinct cultural and ethnic groups divided into communities with strong local identities and fierce local rivalries (if you want more on this, check out Salmon, The Making of Roman Italy (1982), Fronda, Between Rome and Carthage (2010), and Keaveney, Rome and the Unification of Italy (2005)).

The Roman army was by no means entirely Roman – it was split between Roman citizens and what the Romans called the socii (lit: ‘allies’) – a polite term for the communities they had subjugated in Italy (a periphery!). Rome demanded military service – this was the resource they would extract – from these communities; the socii pretty much always made up more than half of the army. Diversity was literally the Roman strength, in terms of total military force. Without it, Rome would have remained just one city-state in Italy, and not a particularly important one besides.

(As an aside: while citizenship is extended to nearly all of Italy in the 80s B.C., by then Rome is making extensive use of non-Italian troops in its armies. by the early empire, half of the army – the auxilia – were non-Roman citizens recruited from the provinces. Roman armies were essentially never majority ‘Roman’ in any period, save possibly for the third century. And before anyone asks what about even earlier than my snapshot – it is quite clear – both archaeologically and in the Roman’s own foundation myths – that Rome was a fusion-society, culturally diverse from the city’s foundation. Indeed, sitting at the meeting point of Latin and Etruscan cultural zones as well as upland and coastal geographic zones was one of the great advantages Rome enjoyed in its early history, as near as we can tell.)

Outside of Italy, narrowly construed, the diversity only increases. Sicily’s population included Greeks, Punic (read: Carthaginian) settlers, and the truly native non-Greeks. Sardinia and Corsica had their own local culture as well. Cisalpine Gaul – the Po River Valley – was, as the name implies, mostly Gallic! As the Romans expended into Spain during the Second Punic War, they would add Iberians, Celt-Iberians, and yet more Punic settlers to their empire. And even those descriptions mask tremendous diversity – Iberians and Celt-Iberians were about as diverse among themselves as the Italians were; a quick read of Strabo reveals a wonderful array of sub-groups in all of these regions, with their own customs, languages, and so on.

Even if the Romans didn’t raise military force directly from any one of these groups, they do need to raise revenue from them – remember, the entire point of having the empire is to raise revenue from it, to make other people do the farming and mining and other labor necessary to support your society from the proceeds of their tribute. To keep that revenue flowing – revenue that, as the Roman army professionalized in the late second century B.C., increasingly paid for Roman military activity which held the empire together – you need to be good at managing those groups. Empires that are bad at handling a wide array of different cultures/religions/languages do not long remain empires.

Conclusion

I bring this up not because I demand utter historical accuracy in my games – I will almost certainly happily play Age of Empires IV despite this. Instead, I bring it up because I find that this overly simplified view – sometimes, depressing, held to be the truth by some – in its errors, reveals some important things about empires, which again, I should note, seem to have been the dominant very-large-scale organization system for humans generally in the historical period.

First it tends to obscure the necessarily diverse nature of empires – as we have just laid out and perhaps more to the point, it obscures the power that empires derive from being diverse. Well-run empires were able to draw on the resources, talents and particular skills of a wide variety of cultures. For instance, the Mongol lifestyle – rooted in the subsistence patterns of the steppe – produced excellence cavalry, but poor infantry and siege skills. But facing a Mongol army often meant facing cutting-edge Chinese siege technology and effective local infantry (whatever infantry the local agrarian societies produced), along with excellent Mongol cavalry. In the same way, a Roman army taking the field at the height of the empire might include not only high quality Italic (read: Roman) infantry, but also Sarmatian or Gallic heavy cavalry, Numidian or Arab light cavalry, expert composite bowmen from the east and skilled light infantrymen from all of the rough country of the empire. I am giving military examples, but the same could be true in engineering, cultural products (which might shore up imperial legitimacy) or administration.

I will say that I find even in games that do pay some lip-service to the continued existence of subordinated peoples within large empires – I think particularly of Europa Universalis, Stellaris and Imperator – the mechanics and dynamics for managing those populations in a polyethnic, polylinguistic society are often sharply underdeveloped. In many games that don’t simply erase those populations, they are instead represented by a simple malus (e.g. “Non-primary culture, -x% production/stability/manpower/whatever). As should already be apparent from the example I gave above, it was often greatly to the advantage of the imperial state to have access to diverse peoples, cultures, climates, etc.

Second, it obscures the necessary hierarchy of empire, by indulging in a fantasy whereby the expansion of an empire means an expansion of its core, rather than the subjugation of a much larger periphery to that core. Until the Constitutio Antoniniana (212 A.D.), most people in the Roman Empire were not Romans and did not enjoy Roman citizenship – they were subjects of the populus Romanus, which was itself a minority within the empire. The dominant human experience of empire is not the experience of being the imperial conqueror – it is the experience of being the subject of empire, of having taxes and resources extracted from you, in the name of a state that likely inflicts violence on you and may care little for your safety or needs.

That’s where we’ll stop this discussion for today – there’s a lot more to talk about in terms of the actual mechanics of how empires function. I have dealt here mostly with the kind of pre-modern empires, tributary empires, and not with the modern sort that we might call mercantilist empires, though the latter are no less diverse than the former (though they could be, on the whole, more brutal and less tolerant). So don’t worry – this is a topic we’ll come back to, though not right away.

But next week, we’re leaping forward – way forward – in time, and asking what the Battlestar Galactica (no, I am not kidding) could learn from the USS South Carolina (BB-26, launched in 1908, and no I am not kidding).

The dominant human experience of empire is not the experience of being the imperial conqueror – it is the experience of being the subject of empire, of having taxes and resources extracted from you, in the name of a state that likely inflicts violence on you and may care little for your safety or needs. That’s where we’ll stop this discussion for today – there’s a lot more to talk about in terms of the actual mechanics of how empires function. I have dealt here mostly with the kind of pre-modern empires, tributary empires, and not with the modern sort that we might call mercantilist empires, though the latter are no less diverse than the former (though they could be, on the whole, more brutal and less tolerant). So don’t worry – this is a topic we’ll come back to, though not right away. But next week, we’re leaping forward – way forward – in time, and asking what the Battlestar Galactica (no, I am not kidding) could learn from the USS South Carolina (BB-26, launched in 1908, and no I am not kidding).

But there’s the thing – those were peoples who had empires. And it turns out that’s a pretty important distinction.

blocks.html_en: What Is an Empire?

sentences.en: What Is an Empire?
sentences.zh: 什么是帝国？

blocks.html_en: Let’s not bury the lede too far: an empire is a state where the core ruling population exercises control and extracts resources from a periphery which is composed of people other than the core group (linguistically/culturally/ethnically/religiously distinct). So an empire is a state where one set of people (the core) extract resources (typically by force) from another set of people (the periphery).

sentences.en: Let’s not bury the lede too far: an empire is a state where the core ruling population exercises control and extracts resources from a periphery which is composed of people other than the core group (linguistically/culturally/ethnically/religiously distinct).
sentences.zh: 我们不要把莱德埋得太深：帝国是一个国家，核心统治人口行使控制权，并从由核心群体以外的人（语言/文化/种族/宗教上不同的）组成的外围地区获取资源。

sentences.en: So an empire is a state where one set of people (the core) extract resources (typically by force) from another set of people (the periphery).
sentences.zh: 因此，帝国是一种国家，其中一组人（核心）从另一组人（外围）那里榨取资源（通常通过武力）。

blocks.html_en: That definition goes back to the root of the word in Latin: imperium, literally meaning a command or control; imperium comes from the Latin verb imperio (lit: ‘to order or command’). Thus imperium was a sphere of command over others. In Roman politics, this could mean an individual had the authority to command an army or to set up courts (consuls, praetors and dictators had this sort of imperium), but the Romans understood their empire as a sort of command exercised by the Senate and People of Rome over non-Roman people, thus they called that too imperium – an imperium of the Roman people (imperium populi Romani), crucially over the non-Roman people; once cannot, after all, have imperium over one’s self. An imperium of the Roman people must be an imperium over someone else.

sentences.en: That definition goes back to the root of the word in Latin: imperium, literally meaning a command or control; imperium comes from the Latin verb imperio (lit: ‘to order or command’).

sentences.en: Thus imperium was a sphere of command over others.
sentences.zh: 因此，帝国是对他人的指挥范围。

sentences.zh: 在罗马政治中，这可能意味着个人有权指挥军队或建立法院（执政官、执政官和独裁者拥有这种统治权），但罗马人将他们的帝国理解为元老院和罗马人民对非罗马人民行使的一种命令，因此他们也称其为帝国——罗马人民的统治权（imperium populi Romani），尤其是对非罗马人民的统治权；毕竟，一旦人无法主宰自己。

sentences.zh: 罗马人民的统治必须是对其他人的统治。

blocks.html_en: Contrary to the venerable Wikipedia, empire does not require a monarchy. Rome was an empire while it was still a Republic, and France continued to hold an empire after it stopped being a monarchy. Athens, famously, converted the Delian League into an Athenian Empire (the Greek word used is ἀρχή (‘arche’), pronounced ar-KHAY) while it was still, internally, a democracy. Often, when discussing the internal politics of these states (especially for Rome and France) we will distinguish between a period of ’empire’ and ‘republic’ to note the shift from a republic to a monarchy or vice-versa, but that sort of nomenclature should not be taken to disguise the fact that, for instance, the Roman Republic in 150 B.C. was very much possessed of an empire, while still functioning as a republic.

sentences.en: Contrary to the venerable Wikipedia, empire does not require a monarchy.
sentences.zh: 与令人尊敬的维基百科相反，帝国不需要君主制。

sentences.en: Rome was an empire while it was still a Republic, and France continued to hold an empire after it stopped being a monarchy.
sentences.zh: 罗马在还是共和国的时候就是一个帝国，而法国在不再是君主制之后仍然拥有一个帝国。

blocks.html_en: Empire, I should note, seems to be one of – if not the – dominant form of large-scale human social organization since at least the bronze age (which is to say: since as far back as our sources let us see clearly). Ideas like loose federations of states (e.g. the EU) or nation-states are relatively new; in many cases, our modern nation-states are merely the consolidated form of what were originally empires of various sizes (e.g. China, Russia, but also France (see: Crusade, Albigensian), etc.). We don’t think about them that way anymore, because the steady application of state power created the shared culture that subsequently formed the foundation for the nation (we’ll return to this – the way that how we think about these empires often disguises their nature – in a moment). In many respects, empire is normal (which, please note, does not mean it is good), whereas this modern world composed primarily of nation-states is an unusual aberration.

sentences.en: Empire, I should note, seems to be one of – if not the – dominant form of large-scale human social organization since at least the bronze age (which is to say: since as far back as our sources let us see clearly).
sentences.zh: 我应该指出，帝国似乎是至少自青铜时代以来（也就是说：早在我们的资料来源就让我们清楚地看到的）大规模人类社会组织的主导形式之一（如果不是的话）。

sentences.en: We don’t think about them that way anymore, because the steady application of state power created the shared culture that subsequently formed the foundation for the nation (we’ll return to this – the way that how we think about these empires often disguises their nature – in a moment).
sentences.zh: 我们不再以这种方式思考它们，因为国家权力的稳定运用创造了共同的文化，随后形成了国家的基础（我们稍后会回到这一点——我们对这些帝国的看法常常掩盖了它们的本质）。

blocks.html_en: (As a side note, this to a degree exposes a bit of understandable but still amusing silliness in titling anything Age of Empires. There has never been an age without empires. We are certainly not living in an age without empires even now – ask the Tibetans, the Uighyrs, the Chechens how ‘ended’ imperialism is! And that doesn’t even get into the sticky question of if we might understand the United States as an empire (my own view is that it is better understood as a hegemony – but I could hardly blame a Guamanian, Puerto Rican or Navajo who begged to differ – and that, importantly, it is also not, and has never been, a nation-state, but that’s a contentious topic for another day).

sentences.zh: （顺便说一句，这在一定程度上暴露了将任何东西命名为“帝国时代”时的一些可以理解但仍然很有趣的愚蠢行为。

sentences.en: There has never been an age without empires.
sentences.zh: 从来没有一个时代没有帝国。

sentences.en: We are certainly not living in an age without empires even now – ask the Tibetans, the Uighyrs, the Chechens how ‘ended’ imperialism is!

blocks.html_en: Which brings us back to Age of Empires, but also a slew of similar games which promise something they call ’empire building.’

sentences.en: Which brings us back to Age of Empires, but also a slew of similar games which promise something they call ’empire building.’
sentences.zh: 这让我们回到了《帝国时代》，但也有很多类似的游戏承诺他们所谓的“帝国建设”。

blocks.html_en: In a typical game of Age of Empires, you begin with a small group of settlers imagined to be founding some great empire. You begin with workers harvesting local resources, which you use to build buildings which in turn produce further workers and then military units (I am, I know, moving over a lot of complexity in terms of tech-trees, age advancements, cheeky things you can do with long-distance trade, wololo and the rest – I do promise I have played these games, but I want to keep to the relevant details).

sentences.en: In a typical game of Age of Empires, you begin with a small group of settlers imagined to be founding some great empire.
sentences.zh: 在典型的帝国时代游戏中，你从一小群定居者开始，想象他们要建立一些伟大的帝国。

sentences.en: You begin with workers harvesting local resources, which you use to build buildings which in turn produce further workers and then military units (I am, I know, moving over a lot of complexity in terms of tech-trees, age advancements, cheeky things you can do with long-distance trade, wololo and the rest – I do promise I have played these games, but I want to keep to the relevant details).
sentences.zh: 你从工人收获当地资源开始，你用这些建筑建造建筑物，进而生产更多工人，然后是军事单位（我知道，在技术树、年龄进步、长途贸易、沃洛洛和其他方面可以做的厚颜无耻的事情方面，我正在经历很多复杂的事情——我保证我玩过这些游戏，但我想保留相关细节）。

blocks.html_en: As the player expands – pursuing one of several victory conditions – they will run into other players (or the AI simulating players). The default setting is for all players to effectively be hostile to each other. Players do not conqueror the settlements of opposing players, or reduce them to tributaries (nor – since only one player or team can ‘win’ – can they settle on mutual cooperation), instead they annihilate those settlements, replacing whatever resource production they had with villager’s of the players own culture. It is striking that villagers cannot normally be captured (we’ll get to priests in a moment!), only killed.

sentences.en: As the player expands – pursuing one of several victory conditions – they will run into other players (or the AI simulating players).
sentences.zh: 随着玩家的扩张——追求几种胜利条件之一——他们会遇到其他玩家（或人工智能模拟玩家）。

sentences.en: The default setting is for all players to effectively be hostile to each other.
sentences.zh: 默认设置是所有玩家实际上彼此敌对。

sentences.en: Players do not conqueror the settlements of opposing players, or reduce them to tributaries (nor – since only one player or team can ‘win’ – can they settle on mutual cooperation), instead they annihilate those settlements, replacing whatever resource production they had with villager’s of the players own culture.
sentences.zh: 玩家不会征服对方玩家的定居点，或将其沦为朝贡国（因为只有一个玩家或团队可以“获胜”，他们也不能决定相互合作），相反，他们会消灭这些定居点，用玩家自己的文化村民的文化取代他们拥有的任何资源生产。

sentences.en: It is striking that villagers cannot normally be captured (we’ll get to priests in a moment!), only killed.
sentences.zh: 令人震惊的是，村民通常不能被捕获（我们稍后会找到牧师！），只能被杀死。

blocks.html_en: This is, to put it bluntly, not how empires work. The entire point of establishing an empire is to access the resources and labor of a subordinate population (the periphery) – exterminating that population defeats the very purpose! The ’empires’ in Age of Empires are not empires at all, but fanatically murderous nation-states, projected backwards in history hundreds – if not thousands – of years before any such idea of a state existed. Few states have really followed this vision of conquest, but those that have – Nazi Germany is the most obvious example – are not generally well-thought of.

sentences.en: This is, to put it bluntly, not how empires work.
sentences.zh: 坦率地说，这不是帝国的运作方式。

sentences.en: The entire point of establishing an empire is to access the resources and labor of a subordinate population (the periphery) – exterminating that population defeats the very purpose!
sentences.zh: 建立帝国的全部目的就是获取从属人口（外围）的资源和劳动力——消灭这些人口就违背了目的！

sentences.en: The ’empires’ in Age of Empires are not empires at all, but fanatically murderous nation-states, projected backwards in history hundreds – if not thousands – of years before any such idea of a state existed.
sentences.zh: 《帝国时代》中的“帝国”根本不是帝国，而是狂热杀戮的民族国家，它们的历史可以追溯到数百年（如果不是数千年）任何国家概念存在之前。

sentences.en: Few states have really followed this vision of conquest, but those that have – Nazi Germany is the most obvious example – are not generally well-thought of.
sentences.zh: 很少有国家真正遵循这种征服愿景，但那些遵循这种征服愿景的国家——纳粹德国是最明显的例子——一般都没有得到很好的考虑。

blocks.html_en: Now, there are three caveats on that rather scathing statement I want to address. First, that the game is to be understood as an abstraction and that we are to imagine all of the complexities of empire building taking place ‘behind the screen’ as it were – and that’s fair enough for many games of this type (it is surely what Civilization seems to want us to believe), but I don’t think it goes for Age of Empires, since the non-combatants are so clearly on screen and are not capturable by military units.

sentences.en: Now, there are three caveats on that rather scathing statement I want to address.
sentences.zh: 现在，我想对这个相当严厉的声明提出三个警告。

sentences.en: First, that the game is to be understood as an abstraction and that we are to imagine all of the complexities of empire building taking place ‘behind the screen’ as it were – and that’s fair enough for many games of this type (it is surely what Civilization seems to want us to believe), but I don’t think it goes for Age of Empires, since the non-combatants are so clearly on screen and are not capturable by military units.
sentences.zh: 首先，游戏应该被理解为一个抽象概念，我们应该想象帝国建设的所有复杂性都发生在“屏幕后面”——这对于许多此类游戏来说是足够公平的（这肯定是《文明》似乎希望我们相信的），但我不认为它适用于《帝国时代》，因为非战斗人员在屏幕上清晰可见，无法被军事单位捕获。

blocks.html_en: They are capturable by priests, which leads to the second caveat – that it is possible to forcibly convert, rather than annihilate, a people in Age of Empires. But that has three problems: first, that sort of cultural genocide is not something real empires usually (there were some exceptions) much cared about (because they do not care what the local customs are, generally, so long as the taxes get paid) and second, that shifting from genocide to ‘mere’ ethnic cleansing is not a great improvement. Third, it is clearly not expected that you will do this to most – or even very many – of your opponents. Priests capture one unit at a time with a long cooldown; the vast majority of humans on the map belonging to rival empires will be killed rather than captured in most games. Capture is merely a niche way to neutralize certain high value enemy units and thus turn battles – mechanically, it is not a real alternative to the war-of-annihilation.

sentences.en: They are capturable by priests, which leads to the second caveat – that it is possible to forcibly convert, rather than annihilate, a people in Age of Empires.

sentences.en: It is safe to assume that, upon seeing this image, anyone who has played the original Age of Empires already has the distinctive ‘Wololo’ chant of this unit stuck in their heads.You’re Welcome.

blocks.html_en: The third, predictable and annoying argument is “stop overthinking it, it is just a game.” Which is – if you will permit me to paraphrase C.S. Lewis – an unserious ‘boy’s philosophy’ unfit for adults. Video games are cultural products, like books or films or any other – and to say that those cultural products have no impact or importance is to argue against the historically obvious, given how frequently we find such things at the roots of important political ideologies, social movements, and wars.

sentences.en: The third, predictable and annoying argument is “stop overthinking it, it is just a game.”
sentences.zh: 第三个可预见且令人恼火的论点是“别想太多了，这只是一个游戏。”

sentences.en: Which is – if you will permit me to paraphrase C.S.
sentences.zh: 这是——如果你允许我解释一下 C.S.

sentences.en: Lewis – an unserious ‘boy’s philosophy’ unfit for adults.
sentences.zh: 刘易斯——一种不严肃的“男孩哲学”，不适合成年人。

sentences.en: Video games are cultural products, like books or films or any other – and to say that those cultural products have no impact or importance is to argue against the historically obvious, given how frequently we find such things at the roots of important political ideologies, social movements, and wars.
sentences.zh: 电子游戏是文化产品，就像书籍、电影或任何其他产品一样——如果说这些文化产品没有影响或重要性，那就是对历史上显而易见的事实的反驳，因为我们经常在重要的政治意识形态、社会运动和战争的根源中发现这些东西。

blocks.html_en: Real Empires Have Peripheries

sentences.en: Real Empires Have Peripheries
sentences.zh: 真正的帝国有外围

blocks.html_en: Alright, so Age of Empires falls down on this account, so what?

sentences.en: Alright, so Age of Empires falls down on this account, so what?
sentences.zh: 好吧，帝国时代就因为这个失败了，那又怎样？

blocks.html_en: I think the problem on this point is that Age of Empires – and cultural products like it – can distort our understanding of what empires are, and how they work and also how our own societies work (when we analogize from past ones – one of the core uses of historical reasoning). In particular, it appears in a failure to appreciate that successful empires historically were diverse by their very nature and that being a successful empire depended on managing diversity and benefiting from it.

sentences.en: I think the problem on this point is that Age of Empires – and cultural products like it – can distort our understanding of what empires are, and how they work and also how our own societies work (when we analogize from past ones – one of the core uses of historical reasoning).
sentences.zh: 我认为这一点上的问题在于，《帝国时代》——以及类似的文化产品——可能会扭曲我们对帝国是什么、它们如何运作以及我们自己的社会如何运作的理解（当我们从过去的社会中进行类比时——这是历史推理的核心用途之一）。

sentences.en: In particular, it appears in a failure to appreciate that successful empires historically were diverse by their very nature and that being a successful empire depended on managing diversity and benefiting from it.
sentences.zh: 特别是，它似乎未能认识到历史上成功的帝国本质上是多样化的，而成为一个成功的帝国取决于管理多样性并从中受益。

blocks.html_en: Let’s take Rome as an example. As an ‘Empire’ in Age of Empires, the Roman player recruits Roman units, destroys their rivals and repopulates the land with Romans (this is not unique to the Romans – all factions function like this in the game). And – I want to make clear this is not a straw-man argument – I have absolutely had well-meaning people quite confidently inform me that Roman military strength was a consequence of their cultural and ethnic cohesiveness. I am not saying they learned this from video games, but rather than their vision and that of a game like Age of Empires seem to coincide and I suspect arise from the same set of misperceptions (yet another word that spellcheck defiantly insists is not a word, even though it is (I link to Websters because the OED is behind a paywall, but rest assured, misperception is there too)).

sentences.en: Let’s take Rome as an example.
sentences.zh: 我们以罗马为例。

sentences.en: As an ‘Empire’ in Age of Empires, the Roman player recruits Roman units, destroys their rivals and repopulates the land with Romans (this is not unique to the Romans – all factions function like this in the game).
sentences.zh: 作为《帝国时代》中的“帝国”，罗马玩家招募罗马单位，消灭他们的对手，并在这片土地上重新布满罗马人（这并不是罗马人独有的——游戏中所有派系都是这样运作的）。

sentences.en: And – I want to make clear this is not a straw-man argument – I have absolutely had well-meaning people quite confidently inform me that Roman military strength was a consequence of their cultural and ethnic cohesiveness.
sentences.zh: 而且——我想澄清这不是稻草人的论点——我绝对有善意的人非常自信地告诉我，罗马的军事实力是他们的文化和种族凝聚力的结果。

sentences.en: I am not saying they learned this from video games, but rather than their vision and that of a game like Age of Empires seem to coincide and I suspect arise from the same set of misperceptions (yet another word that spellcheck defiantly insists is not a word, even though it is (I link to Websters because the OED is behind a paywall, but rest assured, misperception is there too)).
sentences.zh: 我并不是说他们从电子游戏中学到了这一点，而是说他们的愿景和《帝国时代》这样的游戏的愿景似乎是一致的，我怀疑这源于同一组误解（拼写检查顽固地坚称另一个单词不是一个单词，尽管它是一个单词（我链接到韦伯斯特，因为 OED 是付费墙后面的，但请放心，误解也存在））。

blocks.html_en: The Age of Empire version of Rome is thus always completely Roman, through and through, and never more powerful than the number of ethnic Romans on the map.

sentences.en: The Age of Empire version of Rome is thus always completely Roman, through and through, and never more powerful than the number of ethnic Romans on the map.
sentences.zh: 因此，《帝国时代》版本的罗马始终是彻头彻尾的罗马人，而且永远不会比地图上罗马人的数量更强大。

blocks.html_en: The actual Roman Empire was fantastically diverse and more importantly, its military success hinged on its diversity at every stage of its existence. In many games and cultural products, that diversity is obscured because we lose sight of ethnic, religious, linguistic and cultural divisions which were very important at the time, but no longer matter to us very much. Let’s take a snapshot of Roman territory in 218 B.C. to give a sense of this.

sentences.en: The actual Roman Empire was fantastically diverse and more importantly, its military success hinged on its diversity at every stage of its existence.
sentences.zh: 实际的罗马帝国极其多样化，更重要的是，它的军事成功取决于其存在的每个阶段的多样性。

sentences.en: In many games and cultural products, that diversity is obscured because we lose sight of ethnic, religious, linguistic and cultural divisions which were very important at the time, but no longer matter to us very much.
sentences.zh: 在许多游戏和文化产品中，这种多样性被掩盖了，因为我们忽视了种族、宗教、语言和文化的差异，这些差异在当时非常重要，但现在对我们来说不再那么重要了。

sentences.en: Let’s take a snapshot of Roman territory in 218 B.C.
sentences.zh: 让我们拍一下公元前 218 年罗马领土的快照。

sentences.en: to give a sense of this.
sentences.zh: 来感受一下这一点。

blocks.html_en: <figcaption>Roman-controlled territory, c. 218 B.C., consisting of Italy proper, along with two provinces, Sicilia (Sicily) and Corsica et Sardinia.  In addition to this, Cisalpine Gaul (the bit of blue north of Ancona) had been just recently been forced under Roman control, but was not yet organized formally as an administrative province.  Roman control of the Illyrian coast was, at this point, extremely limited – maps have a way of overstating the extent of imperial control.</figcaption>

sentences.en: Roman-controlled territory, c.
sentences.zh: 罗马控制的领土，c。

sentences.en: 218 B.C., consisting of Italy proper, along with two provinces, Sicilia (Sicily) and Corsica et Sardinia.
sentences.zh: 公元前218年，由意大利本土以及两个省份组成：西西里岛（西西里岛）和科西嘉岛和撒丁岛。

sentences.en: In addition to this, Cisalpine Gaul (the bit of blue north of Ancona) had been just recently been forced under Roman control, but was not yet organized formally as an administrative province.
sentences.zh: 除此之外，山南高卢（安科纳以北的那片蓝色）最近才被迫置于罗马控制之下，但尚未正式组建为一个行政省。

sentences.en: Roman control of the Illyrian coast was, at this point, extremely limited – maps have a way of overstating the extent of imperial control.
sentences.zh: 此时，罗马对伊利里亚海岸的控制极其有限——地图总是夸大帝国控制的范围。

blocks.html_en: Quite a few people look at a map like that, classify most of Rome’s territories as ‘Italian’ and assume there is a large, homogeneous ethnic core there (except, I suspect, anyone who has actually been to Italy and is aware that Italy is hardly homogeneous, even today!). But Roman Italy in 218 B.C. was nothing like that.

sentences.en: Quite a few people look at a map like that, classify most of Rome’s territories as ‘Italian’ and assume there is a large, homogeneous ethnic core there (except, I suspect, anyone who has actually been to Italy and is aware that Italy is hardly homogeneous, even today!).
sentences.zh: 很多人看着这样的地图，将罗马的大部分领土归类为“意大利”，并假设那里有一个庞大的、同质的种族核心（除了，我怀疑，任何真正去过意大利并意识到意大利即使在今天也很难同质的人除外！）。

sentences.en: But Roman Italy in 218 B.C.
sentences.zh: 但公元前 218 年的罗马意大利

sentences.en: was nothing like that.
sentences.zh: 根本不是那样的。

blocks.html_en: Peninsular Italy (which doesn’t include the Po River Valley) contained a bewildering array of cultures and peoples: at least three distinct religious systems (Roman, Etruscan, Greek), half a dozen languages (some completely unrelated to each other) and many clearly distinct cultural and ethnic groups divided into communities with strong local identities and fierce local rivalries (if you want more on this, check out Salmon, The Making of Roman Italy (1982), Fronda, Between Rome and Carthage (2010), and Keaveney, Rome and the Unification of Italy (2005)).

sentences.en: Peninsular Italy (which doesn’t include the Po River Valley) contained a bewildering array of cultures and peoples: at least three distinct religious systems (Roman, Etruscan, Greek), half a dozen languages (some completely unrelated to each other) and many clearly distinct cultural and ethnic groups divided into communities with strong local identities and fierce local rivalries (if you want more on this, check out Salmon, The Making of Roman Italy (1982), Fronda, Between Rome and Carthage (2010), and Keaveney, Rome and the Unification of Italy (2005)).
sentences.zh: 意大利半岛（不包括波河流域）包含着一系列令人眼花缭乱的文化和民族：至少三种不同的宗教体系（罗马、伊特鲁里亚、希腊）、六种语言（有些彼此完全无关）以及许多明显不同的文化和种族群体，这些群体划分为具有强烈的地方认同和激烈的地方竞争的社区（如果你想了解更多关于这一点，请查看萨蒙，《罗马意大利的形成》（1982），《弗隆达》，《罗马与迦太基之间》） （2010）和基文尼，《罗马与意大利的统一》（2005））。

blocks.html_en: The Roman army was by no means entirely Roman – it was split between Roman citizens and what the Romans called the socii (lit: ‘allies’) – a polite term for the communities they had subjugated in Italy (a periphery!). Rome demanded military service – this was the resource they would extract – from these communities; the socii pretty much always made up more than half of the army. Diversity was literally the Roman strength, in terms of total military force. Without it, Rome would have remained just one city-state in Italy, and not a particularly important one besides.

sentences.en: The Roman army was by no means entirely Roman – it was split between Roman citizens and what the Romans called the socii (lit: ‘allies’) – a polite term for the communities they had subjugated in Italy (a periphery!).
sentences.zh: 罗马军队绝不完全是罗马人的——它分为罗马公民和罗马人所说的“socii”（字面意思：“盟友”）——这是对他们在意大利征服的社区（外围！）的礼貌用语。

sentences.en: Rome demanded military service – this was the resource they would extract – from these communities; the socii pretty much always made up more than half of the army.
sentences.zh: 罗马要求从这些社区服兵役——这是他们要榨取的资源；社会团体几乎总是占军队的一半以上。

sentences.en: Diversity was literally the Roman strength, in terms of total military force.
sentences.zh: 就总军事力量而言，多样性实际上是罗马的优势。

sentences.en: Without it, Rome would have remained just one city-state in Italy, and not a particularly important one besides.
sentences.zh: 如果没有它，罗马将只是意大利的一个城邦，而不是一个特别重要的城邦。

blocks.html_en: (As an aside: while citizenship is extended to nearly all of Italy in the 80s B.C., by then Rome is making extensive use of non-Italian troops in its armies. by the early empire, half of the army – the auxilia – were non-Roman citizens recruited from the provinces. Roman armies were essentially never majority ‘Roman’ in any period, save possibly for the third century. And before anyone asks what about even earlier than my snapshot – it is quite clear – both archaeologically and in the Roman’s own foundation myths – that Rome was a fusion-society, culturally diverse from the city’s foundation. Indeed, sitting at the meeting point of Latin and Etruscan cultural zones as well as upland and coastal geographic zones was one of the great advantages Rome enjoyed in its early history, as near as we can tell.)

sentences.en: (As an aside: while citizenship is extended to nearly all of Italy in the 80s B.C., by then Rome is making extensive use of non-Italian troops in its armies.
sentences.zh: （顺便说一句：虽然在公元前 80 年代，公民身份几乎扩展到整个意大利，但那时罗马正在其军队中广泛使用非意大利军队。

sentences.en: by the early empire, half of the army – the auxilia – were non-Roman citizens recruited from the provinces.
sentences.zh: 在帝国早期，一半的军队（即辅助军）是从各行省招募的非罗马公民。

sentences.en: Roman armies were essentially never majority ‘Roman’ in any period, save possibly for the third century.
sentences.zh: 在任何时期，罗马军队基本上都不是以“罗马人”为主，可能除了三世纪。

sentences.en: And before anyone asks what about even earlier than my snapshot – it is quite clear – both archaeologically and in the Roman’s own foundation myths – that Rome was a fusion-society, culturally diverse from the city’s foundation.
sentences.zh: 在有人问起甚至早于我的快照之前——无论是在考古学上还是在罗马自己的基础神话中——都很清楚——罗马是一个融合社会，在文化上与这座城市的基础是不同的。

sentences.en: Indeed, sitting at the meeting point of Latin and Etruscan cultural zones as well as upland and coastal geographic zones was one of the great advantages Rome enjoyed in its early history, as near as we can tell.)
sentences.zh: 事实上，据我们所知，位于拉丁文化区和伊特鲁里亚文化区以及高地和沿海地理区域的交汇点是罗马在其早期历史中享有的巨大优势之一。）

blocks.html_en: Outside of Italy, narrowly construed, the diversity only increases. Sicily’s population included Greeks, Punic (read: Carthaginian) settlers, and the truly native non-Greeks. Sardinia and Corsica had their own local culture as well. Cisalpine Gaul – the Po River Valley – was, as the name implies, mostly Gallic! As the Romans expended into Spain during the Second Punic War, they would add Iberians, Celt-Iberians, and yet more Punic settlers to their empire. And even those descriptions mask tremendous diversity – Iberians and Celt-Iberians were about as diverse among themselves as the Italians were; a quick read of Strabo reveals a wonderful array of sub-groups in all of these regions, with their own customs, languages, and so on.

sentences.en: Outside of Italy, narrowly construed, the diversity only increases.
sentences.zh: 在狭义的意大利之外，多样性只会增加。

sentences.en: Sicily’s population included Greeks, Punic (read: Carthaginian) settlers, and the truly native non-Greeks.
sentences.zh: 西西里岛的人口包括希腊人、布匿（读作：迦太基）定居者和真正的本土非希腊人。

sentences.en: Sardinia and Corsica had their own local culture as well.
sentences.zh: 撒丁岛和科西嘉岛也有自己的当地文化。

sentences.en: Cisalpine Gaul – the Po River Valley – was, as the name implies, mostly Gallic!
sentences.zh: 山南高卢——波河流域——顾名思义，主要是高卢人！

sentences.en: As the Romans expended into Spain during the Second Punic War, they would add Iberians, Celt-Iberians, and yet more Punic settlers to their empire.
sentences.zh: 随着罗马人在第二次布匿战争期间向西班牙扩张，他们将伊比利亚人、凯尔特伊比利亚人以及更多的布匿定居者纳入他们的帝国。

sentences.en: And even those descriptions mask tremendous diversity – Iberians and Celt-Iberians were about as diverse among themselves as the Italians were; a quick read of Strabo reveals a wonderful array of sub-groups in all of these regions, with their own customs, languages, and so on.
sentences.zh: 甚至这些描述也掩盖了巨大的多样性——伊比利亚人和凯尔特-伊比利亚人之间的多样性就像意大利人一样；快速阅读斯特拉博就会发现所有这些地区都有一系列精彩的子群体，它们有自己的习俗、语言等等。

blocks.html_en: Even if the Romans didn’t raise military force directly from any one of these groups, they do need to raise revenue from them – remember, the entire point of having the empire is to raise revenue from it, to make other people do the farming and mining and other labor necessary to support your society from the proceeds of their tribute. To keep that revenue flowing – revenue that, as the Roman army professionalized in the late second century B.C., increasingly paid for Roman military activity which held the empire together – you need to be good at managing those groups. Empires that are bad at handling a wide array of different cultures/religions/languages do not long remain empires.

sentences.en: Even if the Romans didn’t raise military force directly from any one of these groups, they do need to raise revenue from them – remember, the entire point of having the empire is to raise revenue from it, to make other people do the farming and mining and other labor necessary to support your society from the proceeds of their tribute.
sentences.zh: 即使罗马人没有直接从这些群体中的任何一个筹集军事力量，他们也确实需要从他们那里增加收入——记住，拥有帝国的全部意义就是从中增加收入，让其他人从事农业、采矿和其他必要的劳动，以从他们的贡品收入中支持你的社会。

sentences.en: To keep that revenue flowing – revenue that, as the Roman army professionalized in the late second century B.C., increasingly paid for Roman military activity which held the empire together – you need to be good at managing those groups.
sentences.zh: 为了保持收入的流动——随着罗马军队在公元前二世纪末的专业化，这些收入越来越多地用于维持帝国团结的罗马军事活动——你需要善于管理这些团体。

sentences.en: Empires that are bad at handling a wide array of different cultures/religions/languages do not long remain empires.
sentences.zh: 不善于处理各种不同文化/宗教/语言的帝国不会长久地成为帝国。

blocks.html_en: Conclusion

sentences.en: Conclusion
sentences.zh: 结论

blocks.html_en: I bring this up not because I demand utter historical accuracy in my games – I will almost certainly happily play Age of Empires IV despite this. Instead, I bring it up because I find that this overly simplified view – sometimes, depressing, held to be the truth by some – in its errors, reveals some important things about empires, which again, I should note, seem to have been the dominant very-large-scale organization system for humans generally in the historical period.

sentences.en: I bring this up not because I demand utter historical accuracy in my games – I will almost certainly happily play Age of Empires IV despite this.
sentences.zh: 我提出这一点并不是因为我要求我的游戏具有完全的历史准确性——尽管如此，我几乎肯定会很高兴地玩《帝国时代 IV》。

sentences.en: Instead, I bring it up because I find that this overly simplified view – sometimes, depressing, held to be the truth by some – in its errors, reveals some important things about empires, which again, I should note, seem to have been the dominant very-large-scale organization system for humans generally in the historical period.
sentences.zh: 相反，我提出这个问题是因为我发现这种过于简化的观点——有时令人沮丧，但被一些人认为是真理——在其错误中揭示了关于帝国的一些重要的事情，我应该再次指出，帝国似乎是整个历史时期人类普遍占主导地位的超大规模组织体系。

blocks.html_en: First it tends to obscure the necessarily diverse nature of empires – as we have just laid out and perhaps more to the point, it obscures the power that empires derive from being diverse. Well-run empires were able to draw on the resources, talents and particular skills of a wide variety of cultures. For instance, the Mongol lifestyle – rooted in the subsistence patterns of the steppe – produced excellence cavalry, but poor infantry and siege skills. But facing a Mongol army often meant facing cutting-edge Chinese siege technology and effective local infantry (whatever infantry the local agrarian societies produced), along with excellent Mongol cavalry. In the same way, a Roman army taking the field at the height of the empire might include not only high quality Italic (read: Roman) infantry, but also Sarmatian or Gallic heavy cavalry, Numidian or Arab light cavalry, expert composite bowmen from the east and skilled light infantrymen from all of the rough country of the empire. I am giving military examples, but the same could be true in engineering, cultural products (which might shore up imperial legitimacy) or administration.

sentences.en: First it tends to obscure the necessarily diverse nature of empires – as we have just laid out and perhaps more to the point, it obscures the power that empires derive from being diverse.
sentences.zh: 首先，它往往会掩盖帝国必然多样化的本质——正如我们刚才所阐述的，也许更切中要害的是，它掩盖了帝国从多样化中获得的力量。

sentences.en: Well-run empires were able to draw on the resources, talents and particular skills of a wide variety of cultures.
sentences.zh: 管理良好的帝国能够利用多种文化的资源、人才和特殊技能。

sentences.en: For instance, the Mongol lifestyle – rooted in the subsistence patterns of the steppe – produced excellence cavalry, but poor infantry and siege skills.
sentences.zh: 例如，植根于草原生存模式的蒙古生活方式造就了优秀的骑兵，但步兵和攻城技术却很差。

sentences.en: But facing a Mongol army often meant facing cutting-edge Chinese siege technology and effective local infantry (whatever infantry the local agrarian societies produced), along with excellent Mongol cavalry.
sentences.zh: 但面对蒙古军队通常意味着面对先进的中国攻城技术和有效的当地步兵（无论当地农业社会生产什么步兵），以及优秀的蒙古骑兵。

sentences.en: In the same way, a Roman army taking the field at the height of the empire might include not only high quality Italic (read: Roman) infantry, but also Sarmatian or Gallic heavy cavalry, Numidian or Arab light cavalry, expert composite bowmen from the east and skilled light infantrymen from all of the rough country of the empire.
sentences.zh: 同样，一支在帝国鼎盛时期上阵的罗马军队可能不仅包括高素质的意大利（读作：罗马）步兵，还包括萨尔马提亚或高卢重骑兵、努米底亚或阿拉伯轻骑兵、来自东方的专家复合弓箭手以及来自帝国各个崎岖国家的熟练轻步兵。

sentences.en: I am giving military examples, but the same could be true in engineering, cultural products (which might shore up imperial legitimacy) or administration.
sentences.zh: 我举的是军事例子，但同样的情况也适用于工程、文化产品（这可能会支撑帝国的合法性）或行政管理。

blocks.html_en: I will say that I find even in games that do pay some lip-service to the continued existence of subordinated peoples within large empires – I think particularly of Europa Universalis, Stellaris and Imperator – the mechanics and dynamics for managing those populations in a polyethnic, polylinguistic society are often sharply underdeveloped. In many games that don’t simply erase those populations, they are instead represented by a simple malus (e.g. “Non-primary culture, -x% production/stability/manpower/whatever). As should already be apparent from the example I gave above, it was often greatly to the advantage of the imperial state to have access to diverse peoples, cultures, climates, etc.

sentences.en: I will say that I find even in games that do pay some lip-service to the continued existence of subordinated peoples within large empires – I think particularly of Europa Universalis, Stellaris and Imperator – the mechanics and dynamics for managing those populations in a polyethnic, polylinguistic society are often sharply underdeveloped.
sentences.zh: 我要说的是，我发现，即使在那些口头上承认大帝国中从属民族继续存在的游戏中——我尤其想到《欧陆风云》、《群星》和《大帝》——在多种族、多语言社会中管理这些人口的机制和动力往往严重不发达。

sentences.en: In many games that don’t simply erase those populations, they are instead represented by a simple malus (e.g. “Non-primary culture, -x% production/stability/manpower/whatever).
sentences.zh: 在许多游戏中，它们并不是简单地消除这些人口，而是用一个简单的恶意来表示（例如“非主要文化，-x% 产量/稳定性/人力/其他）。

sentences.en: As should already be apparent from the example I gave above, it was often greatly to the advantage of the imperial state to have access to diverse peoples, cultures, climates, etc.
sentences.zh: 从我上面举的例子中已经可以明显看出，帝国国家能够接触到不同的民族、文化、气候等往往具有很大的优势。

blocks.html_en: Second, it obscures the necessary hierarchy of empire, by indulging in a fantasy whereby the expansion of an empire means an expansion of its core, rather than the subjugation of a much larger periphery to that core. Until the Constitutio Antoniniana (212 A.D.), most people in the Roman Empire were not Romans and did not enjoy Roman citizenship – they were subjects of the populus Romanus, which was itself a minority within the empire. The dominant human experience of empire is not the experience of being the imperial conqueror – it is the experience of being the subject of empire, of having taxes and resources extracted from you, in the name of a state that likely inflicts violence on you and may care little for your safety or needs.

sentences.en: Second, it obscures the necessary hierarchy of empire, by indulging in a fantasy whereby the expansion of an empire means an expansion of its core, rather than the subjugation of a much larger periphery to that core.
sentences.zh: 其次，它掩盖了帝国必要的等级制度，因为它沉迷于一种幻想，即帝国的扩张意味着其核心的扩张，而不是一个更大的外围国家对这个核心的征服。

sentences.en: Until the Constitutio Antoniniana (212 A.D.), most people in the Roman Empire were not Romans and did not enjoy Roman citizenship – they were subjects of the populus Romanus, which was itself a minority within the empire.
sentences.zh: 在《安东尼宪法》（公元 212 年）颁布之前，罗马帝国的大多数人都不是罗马人，也不享有罗马公民身份——他们是罗马民众的臣民，而罗马民众本身就是帝国内的少数群体。

sentences.en: The dominant human experience of empire is not the experience of being the imperial conqueror – it is the experience of being the subject of empire, of having taxes and resources extracted from you, in the name of a state that likely inflicts violence on you and may care little for your safety or needs.
sentences.zh: 人类对帝国的主要体验不是成为帝国征服者的体验，而是成为帝国臣民的体验，以一个可能对你施加暴力并且几乎不关心你的安全或需求的国家的名义从你身上榨取税收和资源的体验。

blocks.html_en: That’s where we’ll stop this discussion for today – there’s a lot more to talk about in terms of the actual mechanics of how empires function. I have dealt here mostly with the kind of pre-modern empires, tributary empires, and not with the modern sort that we might call mercantilist empires, though the latter are no less diverse than the former (though they could be, on the whole, more brutal and less tolerant). So don’t worry – this is a topic we’ll come back to, though not right away.

sentences.en: That’s where we’ll stop this discussion for today – there’s a lot more to talk about in terms of the actual mechanics of how empires function.
sentences.zh: 这就是我们今天讨论的地方——关于帝国运作的实际机制，还有很多东西要讨论。

sentences.en: I have dealt here mostly with the kind of pre-modern empires, tributary empires, and not with the modern sort that we might call mercantilist empires, though the latter are no less diverse than the former (though they could be, on the whole, more brutal and less tolerant).
sentences.zh: 我在这里主要讨论的是前现代帝国、朝贡帝国，而不是我们可以称之为重商主义帝国的现代帝国，尽管后者的多样性并不比前者差（尽管它们总体上可能更残酷、更不宽容）。

sentences.en: So don’t worry – this is a topic we’ll come back to, though not right away.
sentences.zh: 所以不用担心——我们会回到这个话题，尽管不是马上。

blocks.html_en: But next week, we’re leaping forward – way forward – in time, and asking what the Battlestar Galactica (no, I am not kidding) could learn from the USS South Carolina (BB-26, launched in 1908, and no I am not kidding).

sentences.zh: 但下周，我们将及时向前迈进，并询问太空堡垒卡拉狄加（不，我不是在开玩笑）可以从南卡罗来纳号航空母舰（BB-26，于 1908 年下水，不，我不是在开玩笑）学到什么。

content_html_zh:
终于，Relic Entertainment 宣布《帝国时代 4》即将推出。 策略游戏玩家有福了！ 我很兴奋——我在 98 年玩过第一款（我可能在这里和自己约会）。

但这个消息让我回到了许多此类游戏的一个常见问题，特别是《帝国时代》：它们实际上没有任何帝国！ 我相信你们中的一些人对这种说法有点困惑——毕竟，游戏里充满了罗马人、蒙古人、波斯人和亚述人，我怎么能说这些不是帝国呢？ 但事情是这样的——那些人拥有帝国。 事实证明这是一个非常重要的区别。

所以今天，我们将讨论什么是帝国，以及（在非常基本的层面上）它们如何运作，以了解此类游戏（不仅是 AoE，还包括文明系列和其他游戏）如何无法反映实际帝国的机制以及为什么这很重要。

什么是帝国？

我们不要把莱德埋得太深：帝国是一个国家，核心统治人口行使控制权，并从由核心群体以外的人（语言/文化/种族/宗教上不同的）组成的外围地区获取资源。 因此，帝国是一种国家，其中一组人（核心）从另一组人（外围）那里榨取资源（通常通过武力）。

这个定义可以追溯到拉丁语这个词的词根：imperium，字面意思是命令或控制； imperium 来自拉丁语动词 imperio（直译：“命令或命令”）。 因此，帝国是对他人的指挥范围。 在罗马政治中，这可能意味着个人有权指挥军队或建立法院（执政官、执政官和独裁者拥有这种统治权），但罗马人将他们的帝国理解为元老院和罗马人民对非罗马人民行使的一种命令，因此他们也称其为帝国——罗马人民的统治权（imperium populi Romani），尤其是对非罗马人民的统治权；毕竟，一旦人无法主宰自己。 罗马人民的统治必须是对其他人的统治。

与令人尊敬的维基百科相反，帝国不需要君主制。 罗马在还是共和国的时候就是一个帝国，而法国在不再是君主制之后仍然拥有一个帝国。 众所周知，雅典将提洛联盟转变为雅典帝国（所使用的希腊词是ἀρχή（“arche”），发音为ar-KHAY），而其内部仍然是一个民主国家。 通常，在讨论这些国家（特别是罗马和法国）的内部政治时，我们会区分“帝国”和“共和”时期，以指出从共和到君主制的转变，反之亦然，但这种术语不应掩盖这样的事实，例如公元前 150 年的罗马共和国。 它在很大程度上拥有一个帝国，同时仍然作为一个共和国运作。

我应该指出，帝国似乎是至少自青铜时代以来（也就是说：早在我们的资料来源就让我们清楚地看到的）大规模人类社会组织的主导形式之一（如果不是的话）。 诸如松散的国家联盟（例如欧盟）或民族国家之类的想法相对较新。在许多情况下，我们的现代民族国家仅仅是最初的各种规模帝国的合并形式（例如中国、俄罗斯，还有法国（参见：十字军东征、阿尔比派）等）。 我们不再以这种方式思考它们，因为国家权力的稳定运用创造了共同的文化，随后形成了国家的基础（我们稍后会回到这一点——我们对这些帝国的看法常常掩盖了它们的本质）。 在许多方面，帝国是正常的（请注意，这并不意味着它是好的），而这个主要由民族国家组成的现代世界是一种不寻常的畸变。

（顺便说一句，这在一定程度上暴露了将任何东西命名为“帝国时代”时的一些可以理解但仍然很有趣的愚蠢行为。 从来没有一个时代没有帝国。 即使现在，我们当然还没有生活在一个没有帝国的时代——问问西藏人、维吾尔人、车臣人，帝国主义是如何“终结”的！ 这甚至没有涉及我们是否可以将美国理解为一个帝国的棘手问题（我自己的观点是，最好将其理解为一个霸权——但我很难责怪那些提出不同意见的关岛人、波多黎各人或纳瓦霍人——而且重要的是，它也不是、也从来不是一个民族国家，但这是另一个有争议的话题，以后再说）。

这让我们回到了《帝国时代》，但也有很多类似的游戏承诺他们所谓的“帝国建设”。

在典型的帝国时代游戏中，你从一小群定居者开始，想象他们要建立一些伟大的帝国。 你从工人收获当地资源开始，你用这些建筑建造建筑物，进而生产更多工人，然后是军事单位（我知道，在技术树、年龄进步、长途贸易、沃洛洛和其他方面可以做的厚颜无耻的事情方面，我正在经历很多复杂的事情——我保证我玩过这些游戏，但我想保留相关细节）。

随着玩家的扩张——追求几种胜利条件之一——他们会遇到其他玩家（或人工智能模拟玩家）。 默认设置是所有玩家实际上彼此敌对。 玩家不会征服对方玩家的定居点，或将其沦为朝贡国（因为只有一个玩家或团队可以“获胜”，他们也不能决定相互合作），相反，他们会消灭这些定居点，用玩家自己的文化村民的文化取代他们拥有的任何资源生产。 令人震惊的是，村民通常不能被捕获（我们稍后会找到牧师！），只能被杀死。

坦率地说，这不是帝国的运作方式。 建立帝国的全部目的就是获取从属人口（外围）的资源和劳动力——消灭这些人口就违背了目的！ 《帝国时代》中的“帝国”根本不是帝国，而是狂热杀戮的民族国家，它们的历史可以追溯到数百年（如果不是数千年）任何国家概念存在之前。 很少有国家真正遵循这种征服愿景，但那些遵循这种征服愿景的国家——纳粹德国是最明显的例子——一般都没有得到很好的考虑。

现在，我想对这个相当严厉的声明提出三个警告。 首先，游戏应该被理解为一个抽象概念，我们应该想象帝国建设的所有复杂性都发生在“屏幕后面”——这对于许多此类游戏来说是足够公平的（这肯定是《文明》似乎希望我们相信的），但我不认为它适用于《帝国时代》，因为非战斗人员在屏幕上清晰可见，无法被军事单位捕获。

他们可以被牧师俘虏，这导致了第二个警告——在《帝国时代》中，有可能强行改变而不是消灭一个民族。 但这存在三个问题：第一，这种文化灭绝通常不是真正的帝国（也有一些例外）非常关心的事情（因为他们通常不关心当地的习俗是什么，只要交税即可）；第二，从种族灭绝转向“纯粹的”种族清洗并不是一个很大的进步。 第三，显然你不会对大多数——甚至很多——你的对手这样做。 牧师一次捕获一个单位，冷却时间很长；在大多数游戏中，地图上属于敌对帝国的绝大多数人类将被杀死而不是被俘虏。 俘虏只是压制某些高价值敌方单位并从而扭转战局的一种小众方式——从机械上讲，它并不是歼灭战的真正替代方案。

第三个可预见且令人恼火的论点是“别想太多了，这只是一个游戏。” 这是——如果你允许我解释一下 C.S. 刘易斯——一种不严肃的“男孩哲学”，不适合成年人。 电子游戏是文化产品，就像书籍、电影或任何其他产品一样——如果说这些文化产品没有影响或重要性，那就是对历史上显而易见的事实的反驳，因为我们经常在重要的政治意识形态、社会运动和战争的根源中发现这些东西。

真正的帝国有外围

好吧，帝国时代就因为这个失败了，那又怎样？

我认为这一点上的问题在于，《帝国时代》——以及类似的文化产品——可能会扭曲我们对帝国是什么、它们如何运作以及我们自己的社会如何运作的理解（当我们从过去的社会中进行类比时——这是历史推理的核心用途之一）。 特别是，它似乎未能认识到历史上成功的帝国本质上是多样化的，而成为一个成功的帝国取决于管理多样性并从中受益。

我们以罗马为例。 作为《帝国时代》中的“帝国”，罗马玩家招募罗马单位，消灭他们的对手，并在这片土地上重新布满罗马人（这并不是罗马人独有的——游戏中所有派系都是这样运作的）。 而且——我想澄清这不是稻草人的论点——我绝对有善意的人非常自信地告诉我，罗马的军事实力是他们的文化和种族凝聚力的结果。 我并不是说他们从电子游戏中学到了这一点，而是说他们的愿景和《帝国时代》这样的游戏的愿景似乎是一致的，我怀疑这源于同一组误解（拼写检查顽固地坚称另一个单词不是一个单词，尽管它是一个单词（我链接到韦伯斯特，因为 OED 是付费墙后面的，但请放心，误解也存在））。

因此，《帝国时代》版本的罗马始终是彻头彻尾的罗马人，而且永远不会比地图上罗马人的数量更强大。

实际的罗马帝国极其多样化，更重要的是，它的军事成功取决于其存在的每个阶段的多样性。 在许多游戏和文化产品中，这种多样性被掩盖了，因为我们忽视了种族、宗教、语言和文化的差异，这些差异在当时非常重要，但现在对我们来说不再那么重要了。 让我们拍一下公元前 218 年罗马领土的快照。 来感受一下这一点。

很多人看着这样的地图，将罗马的大部分领土归类为“意大利”，并假设那里有一个庞大的、同质的种族核心（除了，我怀疑，任何真正去过意大利并意识到意大利即使在今天也很难同质的人除外！）。 但公元前 218 年的罗马意大利 根本不是那样的。

意大利半岛（不包括波河流域）包含着一系列令人眼花缭乱的文化和民族：至少三种不同的宗教体系（罗马、伊特鲁里亚、希腊）、六种语言（有些彼此完全无关）以及许多明显不同的文化和种族群体，这些群体划分为具有强烈的地方认同和激烈的地方竞争的社区（如果你想了解更多关于这一点，请查看萨蒙，《罗马意大利的形成》（1982），《弗隆达》，《罗马与迦太基之间》） （2010）和基文尼，《罗马与意大利的统一》（2005））。

罗马军队绝不完全是罗马人的——它分为罗马公民和罗马人所说的“socii”（字面意思：“盟友”）——这是对他们在意大利征服的社区（外围！）的礼貌用语。 罗马要求从这些社区服兵役——这是他们要榨取的资源；社会团体几乎总是占军队的一半以上。 就总军事力量而言，多样性实际上是罗马的优势。 如果没有它，罗马将只是意大利的一个城邦，而不是一个特别重要的城邦。

（顺便说一句：虽然在公元前 80 年代，公民身份几乎扩展到整个意大利，但那时罗马正在其军队中广泛使用非意大利军队。 在帝国早期，一半的军队（即辅助军）是从各行省招募的非罗马公民。 在任何时期，罗马军队基本上都不是以“罗马人”为主，可能除了三世纪。 在有人问起甚至早于我的快照之前——无论是在考古学上还是在罗马自己的基础神话中——都很清楚——罗马是一个融合社会，在文化上与这座城市的基础是不同的。 事实上，据我们所知，位于拉丁文化区和伊特鲁里亚文化区以及高地和沿海地理区域的交汇点是罗马在其早期历史中享有的巨大优势之一。）

在狭义的意大利之外，多样性只会增加。 西西里岛的人口包括希腊人、布匿（读作：迦太基）定居者和真正的本土非希腊人。 撒丁岛和科西嘉岛也有自己的当地文化。 山南高卢——波河流域——顾名思义，主要是高卢人！ 随着罗马人在第二次布匿战争期间向西班牙扩张，他们将伊比利亚人、凯尔特伊比利亚人以及更多的布匿定居者纳入他们的帝国。 甚至这些描述也掩盖了巨大的多样性——伊比利亚人和凯尔特-伊比利亚人之间的多样性就像意大利人一样；快速阅读斯特拉博就会发现所有这些地区都有一系列精彩的子群体，它们有自己的习俗、语言等等。

即使罗马人没有直接从这些群体中的任何一个筹集军事力量，他们也确实需要从他们那里增加收入——记住，拥有帝国的全部意义就是从中增加收入，让其他人从事农业、采矿和其他必要的劳动，以从他们的贡品收入中支持你的社会。 为了保持收入的流动——随着罗马军队在公元前二世纪末的专业化，这些收入越来越多地用于维持帝国团结的罗马军事活动——你需要善于管理这些团体。 不善于处理各种不同文化/宗教/语言的帝国不会长久地成为帝国。

结论

我提出这一点并不是因为我要求我的游戏具有完全的历史准确性——尽管如此，我几乎肯定会很高兴地玩《帝国时代 IV》。 相反，我提出这个问题是因为我发现这种过于简化的观点——有时令人沮丧，但被一些人认为是真理——在其错误中揭示了关于帝国的一些重要的事情，我应该再次指出，帝国似乎是整个历史时期人类普遍占主导地位的超大规模组织体系。

首先，它往往会掩盖帝国必然多样化的本质——正如我们刚才所阐述的，也许更切中要害的是，它掩盖了帝国从多样化中获得的力量。 管理良好的帝国能够利用多种文化的资源、人才和特殊技能。 例如，植根于草原生存模式的蒙古生活方式造就了优秀的骑兵，但步兵和攻城技术却很差。 但面对蒙古军队通常意味着面对先进的中国攻城技术和有效的当地步兵（无论当地农业社会生产什么步兵），以及优秀的蒙古骑兵。 同样，一支在帝国鼎盛时期上阵的罗马军队可能不仅包括高素质的意大利（读作：罗马）步兵，还包括萨尔马提亚或高卢重骑兵、努米底亚或阿拉伯轻骑兵、来自东方的专家复合弓箭手以及来自帝国各个崎岖国家的熟练轻步兵。 我举的是军事例子，但同样的情况也适用于工程、文化产品（这可能会支撑帝国的合法性）或行政管理。

我要说的是，我发现，即使在那些口头上承认大帝国中从属民族继续存在的游戏中——我尤其想到《欧陆风云》、《群星》和《大帝》——在多种族、多语言社会中管理这些人口的机制和动力往往严重不发达。 在许多游戏中，它们并不是简单地消除这些人口，而是用一个简单的恶意来表示（例如“非主要文化，-x% 产量/稳定性/人力/其他）。 从我上面举的例子中已经可以明显看出，帝国国家能够接触到不同的民族、文化、气候等往往具有很大的优势。

其次，它掩盖了帝国必要的等级制度，因为它沉迷于一种幻想，即帝国的扩张意味着其核心的扩张，而不是一个更大的外围国家对这个核心的征服。 在《安东尼宪法》（公元 212 年）颁布之前，罗马帝国的大多数人都不是罗马人，也不享有罗马公民身份——他们是罗马民众的臣民，而罗马民众本身就是帝国内的少数群体。 人类对帝国的主要体验不是成为帝国征服者的体验，而是成为帝国臣民的体验，以一个可能对你施加暴力并且几乎不关心你的安全或需求的国家的名义从你身上榨取税收和资源的体验。

这就是我们今天讨论的地方——关于帝国运作的实际机制，还有很多东西要讨论。 我在这里主要讨论的是前现代帝国、朝贡帝国，而不是我们可以称之为重商主义帝国的现代帝国，尽管后者的多样性并不比前者差（尽管它们总体上可能更残酷、更不宽容）。 所以不用担心——我们会回到这个话题，尽管不是马上。

但下周，我们将及时向前迈进，并询问太空堡垒卡拉狄加（不，我不是在开玩笑）可以从南卡罗来纳号航空母舰（BB-26，于 1908 年下水，不，我不是在开玩笑）学到什么。
